perturbation.original_text:
Auditor affirms that it shall:
( a) Use the Confidential Information as necessary only in connection with scope of audit and in accordance with the terms and conditions contained herein
perturbation.changed_text:
Auditor affirms that it shall:
( a) Endeavor to use the Confidential Information as necessary in connection with the scope of audit and in general accordance with the terms and conditions contained herein
perturbation.explanation: The addition of "endeavor to" and "in general accordance" weakens the obligation to use confidential information solely within the scope of the audit. It introduces a level of discretion that may allow the auditor to use the information outside of the agreed-upon terms, making the obligation ambiguous and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 72 of the Information Technology Act, 2000
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 72 of the Information Technology Act, 2000, addresses the breach of confidentiality and privacy. Specifically, if any person who, in pursuance of any of the powers conferred by this Act, rules or regulations made thereunder, has secured access to any electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material without the consent of the person concerned discloses such electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material to any other person shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both. The original text is very clear on what is allowed, where the modified text allows for the use of this information beyond this.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: India Code: Invalid URL or Argument(s). Invalid URL or Argument(s). The system has experienced an error. Please try to do what you were doing again, and if the problem persists, please contact us so we can fix the problem. Go to the DSpace home page
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates specific use of confidential information, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the auditor will "endeavor to use" and act in "general accordance." This weakening contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation, which, referencing Section 72 of the Information Technology Act, 2000, emphasizes the consequences of unauthorized disclosure of information. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 experienced an error, the Law Explanation's interpretation of Section 72, coupled with the weakening of the obligation in the changed text, suggests a potential contradiction of legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: Not to send Auditee’s audit information or data and/or any such Confidentia l Information at any time outside India for the purpose of storage, processing, analysis or handling without the express written consent of the Auditee.
perturbation.changed_text: Not to routinely send Auditee’s audit information or data and/or any such Confidentia l Information outside India for the purpose of storage, processing, analysis or handling without the express written consent of the Auditee.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of "routinely" makes the obligation vague by implying that occasional sending of data outside India may be acceptable without explicit consent, which could potentially contradict data localization requirements and creates an ambiguity.
perturbation.law_citation: Clause 34 of the Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the PDP Bill, 2019 was withdrawn, the underlying principle is still maintained under the new Digital Personal Data Protection Act of 2023 and IT Act 2000. This clause addressed cross-border transfer of personal data. While the bill aimed for specific regulations and restrictions of this data, allowing for potentially routine transfers without explicit consent of the Auditee contradicts the spirit of data localization and protection efforts. 'Routinely' is ambiguous and subjective, making it difficult to prove a violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Data Protection Bill of India This article is about an earlier proposed Data Protection legislation by the Government of India which was withdrawn. For the present Data Protection Law, see Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 . Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019 Parliament of India Long title Personal Data Protection Bill 2019 Territorial extent India Enacted by Parliament of India Legislative history Introduced by Ravi Shankar Prasad Minister of Electronics and Information Introduced 11 December 2019 Committee report Joint Parliamentary Committee (JPC) on Personal Data Protection Status: Withdrawn The Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019 (PDP Bill 2019) was a proposed legislation by the Parliament of India which was withdrawn. The bill covers mechanisms for protection of personal data and proposes the setting up of a Data Protection Authority of India for the same. [ 1 ] Some key provisions the 2019 Bill provides for which the 2018 draft Bill did not, such as that the central government can exempt any government agency from the Bill and the Right to Be Forgotten , have been included. [ 2 ] [ 3 ] Background and timeline [ edit ] In July 2017, the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology set up a committee to study issues related to data protection. The committee was chaired by retired Supreme Court judge Justice B. N. Srikrishna . [ 4 ] The committee submitted the draft Personal Data Protection Bill, 2018 [ 5 ] in July 2018. [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] After further deliberations the Bill was approved by the cabinet ministry of India on 4 December 2019 [ 9 ] [ 10 ] as the Personal Data Protection Bill 2019 and tabled in the Lok Sabha on 11 December 2019. [ 1 ] In March 2020 the Bill was being analyzed by a Joint Parliamentary Committee (JPC) in consultation with experts and stakeholders. The JPC, which was set up in December 2019, was headed by Meenakshi Lekhi , Member of Parliament . While the
perturbation.justification: The addition of "routinely" introduces ambiguity regarding the frequency with which Auditee's data can be sent outside India without express written consent. This ambiguity undermines the principle of data localization and protection, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, by potentially allowing occasional data transfers without explicit consent. While the scraped snippets primarily discuss the Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019 (which was withdrawn), the Law Explanation asserts that the underlying principle of data protection is maintained under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act of 2023 and the IT Act 2000. The change creates an ambiguous legal obligation, making it difficult to enforce the original intent of restricting data transfer outside India, meeting the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The auditor shall use only the best possible secure methodology to avoid confidentiality breach, while handling audit related data for the purpose of storage, processing, transit or analysis including sharing of information with auditee.
perturbation.changed_text: The auditor shall endeavor to use what they consider a reasonably secure methodology to avoid confidentiality breach, while handling audit related data for the purpose of storage, processing, transit or analysis including sharing of information with auditee.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "best possible secure methodology" to "what they consider a reasonably secure methodology" introduces ambiguity. The phrase "reasonably secure methodology" is subjective, and allows the auditor to use their own judgment. This contradicts laws requiring specific security standards to protect confidential data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 4 of the Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 4 of the Information Technology (Reasonable Security Practices and Procedures and Sensitive Personal Data or Information) Rules, 2011, outlines the security practices that companies must adhere to when handling sensitive personal data. Replacing 'best possible' with a subjective standard like 'reasonably secure' potentially falls short of the prescribed security measures, making it difficult to determine compliance with the rule and opens a loophole for the auditor to employ security measures that are not up to par.
perturbation.justification: The change from "best possible secure methodology" to "what they consider a reasonably secure methodology" introduces ambiguity and weakens the security obligation. The original text mandates a high standard, while the changed text allows the auditor's subjective judgment. The law explanation states that replacing "best possible" with a subjective standard like "reasonably secure" potentially falls short of prescribed security measures. This aligns with the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Auditor acknowledges that any actual or threatened disclosure or use of the Confidential Information by Aud itor would be a breach of this agreement and may cause immediate and irreparable harm to Auditee or to its clients; Auditor affirms that damages from such disclosure or use by it may be impossible to measure accurately; and injury sustained by Auditee / it s clients may be impossible to calculate and compensate fully. Therefore, Auditor acknowledges that in the event of such a breach, Auditee shall be entitled to specific performance by Auditor of its obligations contained in this Agreement. In addition , Auditor shall compensate the Auditee for the loss or damages caused to the auditee , actual and liquidated damages , which may be demanded by Auditee with l iquidated damages not to exceed the Contract value.
perturbation.changed_text: Auditor acknowledges that any actual disclosure or use of the Confidential Information by Auditor would be a breach of this agreement; Auditor believes that damages from such disclosure or use by it may be difficult to measure accurately; and injury sustained by Auditee / it s clients may be challenging to calculate and compensate fully. Therefore, Auditor acknowledges that in the event of such a breach, Auditee may be entitled to seek specific performance by Auditor of its obligations contained in this Agreement. In addition, Auditor may compensate the Auditee for the direct loss or damages caused to the auditee , which may be demanded by Auditee with liquidated damages not to exceed a mutually agreed amount.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the remedies available to the Auditee in case of a breach. Replacing 'may cause immediate and irreparable harm' with the Auditor's belief that 'damages may be difficult to measure' and replacing 'Auditee shall be entitled' with 'Auditee may be entitled' weakens the enforcement of the remedies and compensation payable. Replacing 'shall compensate' with 'may compensate' makes the compensation discretionary, undermining the seriousness of a breach. Finally, "mutually agreed amount" creates a disagreement that didn't exist before.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_citation: Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive, from the party who has broken the contract, compensation for any loss or damage caused to him thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it. By making the compensation discretionary ('may compensate'), the modified clause contradicts the entitlement to compensation as per Section 73, thus creating an ambiguous and unenforceable obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Compensation of loss or damage caused by breach of contract | Indian Contract Act, 1872 | Bare Acts | Law Library | AdvocateKhoj Follow @SCJudgments Login : Advocate | Client Indian Contract Act, 1872 73. Compensation of loss or damage caused by breach of contract When a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive, from the party who has broken the contract, compensation for any loss or damage caused to him thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it. Such compensation is not to be given for any remote and indirect loss or damage sustained by reason of the breach. Compensation for failure to discharge obligation resembling those created by contract: When an obligation resembling those created by contract has been incurred and has not been discharged, any person injured by the failure to discharge it is entitled to receive the same compensation from the party in default, as if such person had contracted to discharge it and had broken his contract. Explanation: In estimating the loss or damage arising from a breach of contract, the means which existed of remedying the inconvenience caused by non-performance of the contract must be taken into account. Illustrations (a) A contracts to sell and deliver 50 maunds of saltpetre to B, at a certain price to be paid on delivery. A breaks his promise. B is entitled to receive from A, by way of compensation, the sum, if any, by which the contract price falls short of the price for which B might have obtained 50 maunds of saltpetre of like quality at the time when the saltpetre ought to have been delivered. (b) A hires B's ship to go to Bombay, and there takes on board, on the first of January, a cargo, which A is to provide, and to bring it to Calcutta, the freight to be paid when earned. B's ship does not go to Bombay, but A has opportunities for procuring suitable conveyance for the cargo upon terms as advantageous as those on which he h
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. The original text mandates compensation ('shall compensate'), whereas the modified text makes it discretionary ('may compensate'). This directly contradicts the law snippet provided, which states that the injured party 'is entitled to receive... compensation'. By making compensation optional, the changed contract undermines the legal entitlement to compensation, thus creating an ambiguous obligation.
